question: What is the fundamental characteristic of a brute force algorithm?
options: It systematically enumerates all possible candidates for a solution.
It always uses the most sophisticated data structures available.
It guarantees the fastest possible execution time for all problems.
It relies on advanced mathematical theorems and proofs to find a solution.
explanation: The core idea of brute force is to solve a problem by exhaustively trying all possible solutions until the correct one is found, without using complex optimizations.

question: For which of the following problems is a brute force approach most practically feasible?
options: Checking if a number is prime by testing divisibility by all integers up to its square root.
Finding the shortest path that visits every city in a 50-city Traveling Salesperson Problem (TSP).
Breaking a 256-bit encryption key by trying all possible combinations.
Sorting a list of one million numbers by generating all possible permutations and checking for sorted order.
explanation: Testing divisibility up to the square root is computationally feasible for most numbers, unlike the other options which have factorial (TSP, sorting) or exponential (encryption) time complexity, making brute force impractical for any non-trivial input size.

question: What is the primary trade-off considered when evaluating a brute force algorithm?
options: Simplicity of design vs. computational efficiency
Optimal solution vs. approximate solution
Memory usage vs. processor cache performance
Worst-case performance vs. best-case performance
explanation: Brute force algorithms are prized for their straightforward, easy-to-implement design. However, this simplicity almost always comes at the cost of high time and/or space complexity, making them inefficient for large problem instances.

question: In the context of the Closest Pair of Points problem, how does the time complexity of the naive brute force algorithm compare to an optimal divide-and-conquer solution?
options: Brute Force is O(n²), while the optimal algorithm is O(n log n).
Brute Force is O(n log n), while the optimal algorithm is O(n).
Both algorithms have a time complexity of O(n²).
Brute Force is O(n!), while the optimal algorithm is O(n²).
explanation: The brute force approach checks every pair of points, resulting in O(n²) time. The efficient solution uses a divide-and-conquer strategy to achieve O(n log n) time.

question: A brute force algorithm is designed to solve the Subset Sum problem. If the input set contains 'n' distinct integers, what is the worst-case time complexity of this approach, and why?
options: O(2ⁿ), because it must potentially examine every possible subset of the input set.
O(n!), because it must generate all permutations of the input set.
O(n²), because it uses a nested loop to compare every pair of elements.
O(n log n), because it first sorts the set and then performs a linear scan.
explanation: The Subset Sum problem requires checking if any subset of the set sums to a target value. There are 2ⁿ possible subsets of a set of size n, leading to exponential worst-case time complexity for a brute force solution that iterates over them all.
